title: Listening - Part 3: The Art of Putting it Off
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
instructions: You will hear an interview with a behavioral psychologist, Dr. Abbi, and a time-management coach, Noah, about why we procrastinate. For questions 15–20, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which fits best.

extracts.context: An interview discussing 'emotional regulation,' the myth of laziness, and the 'Zeigarnik Effect.'
extracts.audio_script:
Interviewer: We’ve all been there: a deadline is looming, but we’re suddenly experts on the history of the Roman Empire or cleaning the fridge. Abbi, why do we do this to ourselves?

Abbi: [15] The biggest misconception is that procrastination is about laziness or poor time management. It’s actually about 'emotional regulation.' [/15] When we face a task that makes us feel anxious, insecure, or overwhelmed, our brain treats that task like a threat. Procrastination is a coping mechanism to avoid those negative feelings in the short term, even if it causes more stress later.

Interviewer: Noah, you help people manage their schedules. Do 'to-do lists' actually help?

Noah: [16] Only if they are used correctly. Most people make lists that are too vague, like 'Write Thesis.' That’s terrifying for the brain. [/16] You have to break it down into 'micro-actions'—things so small they don't trigger the fear response. Instead of 'Write Thesis,' try 'Open a blank document and type one sentence.' Once you start, the 'Zeigarnik Effect' kicks in—our brain naturally wants to finish what we’ve begun.

Interviewer: Abbi, what is the role of 'perfectionism' in this?

Abbi: [17] Perfectionism is the mother of procrastination. If you believe the result must be perfect, the pressure becomes paralyzing. [/17] It’s safer for the ego to have 'failed because I didn't have time' than to have 'failed because I’m not good enough.' We have to learn to embrace 'the messy first draft.'

Interviewer: Noah, how does technology influence our ability to stay on task?

Noah: [18] It’s a disaster for our 'deep work' capabilities. Apps are designed to give us instant gratification, which is the opposite of a difficult, long-term project. [/18] We are constantly tempted by the 'low-effort, high-reward' stimulus of social media, making the 'high-effort, delayed-reward' of our work feel unbearable.

Interviewer: Abbi, is there a way to 'cure' procrastination?

Abbi: [19] It’s not about willpower; it’s about 'self-compassion.' Research shows that students who forgave themselves for procrastinating on the first exam actually studied more for the second one. [/19] Shaming yourself just adds more negative emotion, which makes you want to avoid the task even more. Be kind to yourself, and then just start.

Interviewer: Final thoughts, Noah? How do we take the first step?

Noah: [20] Use the 'Five-Minute Rule.' Tell yourself you will work for only five minutes and then you can stop. Usually, once the friction of starting is gone, you’ll keep going. [/20]

Interviewer: Abbi, Noah, thank you.

questions.question: Dr. Abbi explains that we procrastinate because our brains
options: A) are naturally lazy and prefer to do nothing.
B) see a difficult task as a threat to our emotional well-being.
C) have not been trained to manage time effectively.
D) prefer to focus on historical facts rather than current work.
questions.explanation: She says it's about 'emotional regulation' and that the brain treats an overwhelming task like a 'threat'.

questions.question: According to Noah, why is the 'Write Thesis' example a bad to-do list item?
options: A) It is too specific and doesn't allow for any creativity.
B) It is a task that should only be done by university professors.
C) It is too broad and triggers a fear response in the brain.
D) It takes several years to complete and cannot be written in a day.
questions.explanation: He says vague, large tasks are 'terrifying' and should be broken into 'micro-actions'.

questions.question: How does perfectionism contribute to procrastination?
options: A) It makes people work too fast and make many mistakes.
B) It creates an unrealistic pressure that prevents a person from starting.
C) It encourages people to finish their work ahead of the deadline.
D) It helps people organize their workspace more efficiently.
questions.explanation: Abbi states that if the result must be perfect, the pressure becomes paralyzing.

questions.question: Noah suggests that modern technology makes work harder because
options: A) the batteries in our devices do not last long enough.
B) it provides instant rewards that compete with long-term goals.
C) computers are much more difficult to use than traditional tools.
D) everyone is forced to work from home using the internet.
questions.explanation: He mentions that apps provide 'instant gratification' which is the opposite of 'delayed-reward' work.

questions.question: Abbi believes that self-forgiveness is important because
options: A) it allows people to stop working and take a long holiday.
B) it reduces the negative emotions that cause avoidance.
C) it proves that procrastination is actually a good thing.
D) it makes other people feel sorry for the procrastinator.
questions.explanation: She says shaming yourself adds negative emotion, while forgiveness encourages future effort.

questions.question: What is the goal of Noah's 'Five-Minute Rule'?
options: A) To finish an entire project in exactly five minutes.
B) To help people decide which task is the most important.
C) To overcome the initial resistance to starting a task.
D) To remind people to take a break every five minutes.
questions.explanation: The rule is meant to get past the 'friction of starting'.
